revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

'''オロチョンの火祭り''' * [[北海道]][[網走市]]でのイベントのひとつとして実施される[[祭|祭り]]。本項で記述。 * [[伊藤久男]]の歌のタイトル。[[1953年]]の[[紅白歌合戦]]で歌われた。
== 概要 == 先住民族の慰霊と豊穣を祈願して行われる[[儀式]]で、[[アイヌ]]の民族衣装に身を包み、[[太鼓]]や[[コロホル]]に合せて炎を囲んで踊る。
== 歴史 == [[オホーツク海]]に面した網走では古来より北方民族との交流が盛んであり、[[モロヨ貝塚]]などでは[[アイヌ民族]]とは異なる[[オホーツク人]]の人骨が発見されている。それらの経緯からモロヨ貝塚において[[1940年]]頃から「モロヨ祭」として北方民族を[[慰霊]]する祭が開催されていた。終戦後の[[1950年]]には[[樺太]]から引揚げてきた[[オロッコ人]]や[[ギリヤーク人]]の協力を得て「オロチョンの火祭り」が正式な市の夏祭りとして組み込まれるようになった。その後[[10月1日]]から実施される「カムバックサーモンin網走湖」に組み込まれ、観光イベントとして人気を博している。
== 名称について == [[オロチョン族|オロチョン]]は元来[[ロシア]]領内に居住する[[ツングース]]系の民族を指す言葉であるが、[[日本]]において「北方民族」を指す言葉として用いられた時期があり、その名残からオロチョンの火祭りと命名されている。ただし、現時点でオロチョン族と呼ばれる民族の文化に火祭りと呼ばれるような儀式は無い。
== 関連項目 == *[[オロチョン族]] *[[網走湖]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://www2s.biglobe.ne.jp/~abashiri/oyado2007/index.html カムバックサーモンin網走湖]（網走市観光協会）

== 概要 == 先住民族の慰霊と豊穣を祈願して行われる[[儀式]]で、[[アイヌ]]の民族衣装に身を包み、[[太鼓]]や[[コロホル]]に合せて炎を囲んで踊る。
== 歴史 == [[オホーツク海]]に面した網走では古来より北方民族との交流が盛んであり、[[モロヨ貝塚]]などでは[[アイヌ民族]]とは異なる[[オホーツク人]]の人骨が発見されている。それらの経緯からモロヨ貝塚において[[1940年]]頃から「モロヨ祭」として北方民族を[[慰霊]]する祭が開催されていた。終戦後の[[1950年]]には[[樺太]]から引揚げてきた[[オロッコ人]]や[[ギリヤーク人]]の協力を得て「オロチョンの火祭り」が正式な市の夏祭りとして組み込まれるようになった。その後[[10月1日]]から実施される「カムバックサーモンin網走湖」に組み込まれ、観光イベントとして人気を博している。
== 名称について == [[オロチョン族|オロチョン]]は元来[[ロシア]]領内に居住する[[ツングース]]系の民族を指す言葉であるが、[[日本]]において「北方民族」を指す言葉として用いられた時期があり、その名残からオロチョンの火祭りと命名されている。ただし、現時点でオロチョン族と呼ばれる民族の文化に火祭りと呼ばれるような儀式は無い。

{{DEFAULTSORT:おろちよんのひまつり}} [[Category:北海道の祭り]]

== 概要 == 先住民族の慰霊と豊穣を祈願して行われる[[儀式]]で、[[アイヌ]]の民族衣装に身を包み、[[太鼓]]や[[コロホル]]に合せて炎を囲んで踊る。
== 歴史 == [[オホーツク海]]に面した網走では古来より北方民族との交流が盛んであり、[[モヨロ貝塚]]などでは[[アイヌ民族]]とは異なる[[オホーツク人]]の人骨が発見されている。それらの経緯からモヨロ貝塚において[[1940年]]頃から「モヨロ祭」として北方民族を[[慰霊]]する祭が開催されていた。終戦後の[[1950年]]には[[樺太]]から引揚げてきた[[ウィルタ]]民族や[[ニブフ]]民族の協力を得て「オロチョンの火祭り」が正式な市の夏祭りとして組み込まれるようになった。その後[[10月1日]]から実施される「カムバックサーモンin網走湖」に組み込まれ、観光イベントとして人気を博している。
== 名称について == [[オロチョン族|オロチョン]]は元来[[ロシア]]領内に居住する[[ツングース]]系の民族を指す言葉であるが、[[日本]]において「北方民族」を指す言葉として用いられた時期があり、その名残からオロチョンの火祭りと命名されている。ただし、現時点でオロチョン族と呼ばれる民族の文化に火祭りと呼ばれるような儀式は無い。
== 関連項目 == *[[オロチョン族]] *[[網走湖]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おろちよんのひまつり}} [[Category:北海道の祭り]] [[Category:網走市]]

revision.comment: Created page with "{{otheruses|Festivals in Hokkaido|the song by Hisao Itō|伊藤久男}} '''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}. The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accomp..."
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Festivals in Hokkaido|the song by Hisao Itō|伊藤久男}} '''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Uilta|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Orochon Fire Festival}} [[Category:Festivals in Hokkaido]] [[Category:Culture in Abashiri]] [[Category:Events established in 1950]]
[[ja:オロチョンの火祭り]]

{{otheruses|Festivals in Hokkaido|the song by Hisao Itō|伊藤久男}} '''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Uilta|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Orochon Fire Festival}} [[Category:Festivals in Hokkaido]] [[Category:Culture in Abashiri]] [[Category:Events established in 1950]]
[[ja:オロチョンの火祭り]]

== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Uilta|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:qq-" to "[[Category:qq deprecated start]]<!--"
revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qq" to "11"
revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.
== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:return the see also]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today.== See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940年}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year }}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. == About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. '''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year }} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year }}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}.

In {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year }}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖}}

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り|12=draft}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|drums|ja|太鼓|12=draft}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル|12=draft}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|ja|オホーツク海|12=draft}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|ja|モヨロ貝塚|12=draft}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人|12=draft}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族|12=draft}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|ja|1940th year|12=draft}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year|12=draft}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|ja|ウィルタ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nivkh|ja|ニヴフ|12=draft}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太|12=draft}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日|12=draft}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon|ja|オロチョン族|12=draft}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|12=draft}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|12=draft}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen_people|lt=Orochon people|ja|オロチョン族|12=draft}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|ja|網走湖|12=draft}}

17 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:オロチョンの火祭り up to rev 95181447
revision.text:
{{otheruses|北海道の祭り|伊藤久男の楽曲|伊藤久男}} '''オロチョンの火祭り'''とは、[[北海道]][[網走市]]でのイベントのひとつとして実施される[[祭|祭り]]である。
先住民族の慰霊と豊穣を祈願して行われる[[儀式]]で、北方少数民族の衣装に身を包み、[[太鼓]]や[[コロホル]]に合せて炎を囲んで踊る。
== 歴史 == [[オホーツク海]]に面した網走では古来より北方民族との交流が盛んであり、[[モヨロ貝塚]]などでは[[アイヌ|アイヌ民族]]の先祖の一部となった[[オホーツク人]]の人骨が発見されている。それらの経緯からモヨロ貝塚において[[1940年]]頃から「モヨロ祭」として北方民族を[[慰霊]]する祭が開催されていた。終戦後の[[1950年]]には[[樺太]]から引揚げてきた[[ウィルタ]]民族や[[ニヴフ|ニブフ]]民族の協力を得て「オロチョンの火祭り」が正式な市の夏祭りとして組み込まれるようになった。その後[[10月1日]]から実施される「カムバックサーモンin網走湖」に組み込まれ、観光イベントとして人気を博している。
== 名称について == [[オロチョン族|オロチョン]]は元来[[ロシア]]領内に居住する[[ツングース]]系の民族を指す言葉であるが、[[日本]]において「北方民族」を指す言葉として漠然と用いられた時期があり、その名残からオロチョンの火祭りと命名されている。ただし、現時点でオロチョン族と呼ばれる民族の文化に火祭りと呼ばれるような儀式は無い。
== 関連項目 == *[[オロチョン族]] *[[網走湖]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おろちよんのひまつり}} [[Category:北海道の祭り]] [[Category:網走市の文化]] [[Category:1950年開始のイベント]]

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festival|qq=}} held as part of the events in [[:en:Abashiri|Abashiri, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]].
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of [[:en:Drum|drums]] and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/khomus|qq=}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the [[:en:Sea of Okhotsk|Sea of Okhotsk]], has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the [[:en:Moyoro Shell Mound|Moyoro Shell Mound]], human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okhotsk people|qq=}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu|qq=}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around [[:en:1940|1940]] to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In [[:en:1950|1950]], after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from [[:en:Oroks|Oroks]] and [[:en:Nivkh people|Nivkh]] peoples who had repatriated from [[:en:Sakhalin|Karafuto]]. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from [[:en:October 1|October 1]], gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "[[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon]]" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tungusic peoples|qq=}} group residing within [[:en:Russia|Russia]]. However, in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * [[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon people]] * [[:en:Lake Abashiri|Lake Abashiri]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:網走市の文化]] into [[Category:Culture of Abashiri City]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭}} held as part of the events in [[:en:Abashiri|Abashiri, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]].
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of [[:en:Drum|drums]] and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/khomus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the [[:en:Sea of Okhotsk|Sea of Okhotsk]], has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the [[:en:Moyoro Shell Mound|Moyoro Shell Mound]], human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okhotsk people|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オホーツク文化}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アイヌ}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around [[:en:1940|1940]] to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In [[:en:1950|1950]], after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from [[:en:Oroks|Oroks]] and [[:en:Nivkh people|Nivkh]] peoples who had repatriated from [[:en:Sakhalin|Karafuto]]. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from [[:en:October 1|October 1]], gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "[[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon]]" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tungusic peoples|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ツングース系民族}} group residing within [[:en:Russia|Russia]]. However, in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * [[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon people]] * [[:en:Lake Abashiri|Lake Abashiri]]

At the [[:en:Moyoro Shell Mound|Moyoro Shell Mound]], human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okhotsk people|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オホーツク文化|1=Okhotsk culture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1093651}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アイヌ|1=Ainu people|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q101828}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around [[:en:1940|1940]] to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In [[:en:1950|1950]], after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from [[:en:Oroks|Oroks]] and [[:en:Nivkh people|Nivkh]] peoples who had repatriated from [[:en:Sakhalin|Karafuto]]. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from [[:en:October 1|October 1]], gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "[[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon]]" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tungusic peoples|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ツングース系民族|1=Tungusic peoples|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q868179}} group residing within [[:en:Russia|Russia]]. However, in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * [[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon people]] * [[:en:Lake Abashiri|Lake Abashiri]]

It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from [[:en:October 1|October 1]], gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "[[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon]]" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tungusic peoples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ツングース系民族|1=Tungusic peoples|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q868179}} group residing within [[:en:Russia|Russia]]. However, in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * [[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon people]] * [[:en:Lake Abashiri|Lake Abashiri]]

At the [[:en:Moyoro Shell Mound|Moyoro Shell Mound]], human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okhotsk people|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オホーツク文化|1=Okhotsk culture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1093651}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アイヌ|1=Ainu people|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q101828}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around [[:en:1940|1940]] to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In [[:en:1950|1950]], after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from [[:en:Oroks|Oroks]] and [[:en:Nivkh people|Nivkh]] peoples who had repatriated from [[:en:Sakhalin|Karafuto]]. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from [[:en:October 1|October 1]], gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "[[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon]]" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tungusic peoples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ツングース系民族|1=Tungusic peoples|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q868179}} group residing within [[:en:Russia|Russia]]. However, in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * [[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon people]] * [[:en:Lake Abashiri|Lake Abashiri]]

== History == Abashiri, located along the [[:en:Sea of Okhotsk|Sea of Okhotsk]], has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the [[:en:Moyoro Shell Mound|Moyoro Shell Mound]], human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okhotsk people|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オホーツク文化|1=Okhotsk culture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1093651}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アイヌ|1=Ainu people|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q101828}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around [[:en:1940|1940]] to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In [[:en:1950|1950]], after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from [[:en:Oroks|Oroks]] and [[:en:Nivkh people|Nivkh]] peoples who had repatriated from [[:en:Sakhalin|Karafuto]]. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from [[:en:October 1|October 1]], gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.
== About the Name == The term "[[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon]]" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tungusic peoples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ツングース系民族|1=Tungusic peoples|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q868179}} group residing within [[:en:Russia|Russia]]. However, in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * [[:en:Oroqen people|Orochon people]] * [[:en:Lake Abashiri|Lake Abashiri]]
{{translated page|ja|オロチョンの火祭り|version=95181447|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:オロチョンの火祭り]]

== About the Name == The term "{{ill|Oroqen people|en|Oroqen people|WD=Q1059053|lt=Orochon}}" originally refers to a {{ill|Tungusic peoples|ja|ツングース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tungusic peoples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ツングース系民族|1=Tungusic peoples|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q868179}} group residing within {{ill|Russia|en|Russia|WD=Q159|lt=Russia}}. However, in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the term was used more broadly to denote "northern peoples" during a certain period. This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen people|en|Oroqen people|WD=Q1059053|lt=Orochon people}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|en|Lake Abashiri|WD=Q572825|lt=Lake Abashiri}}

'''Orochon Fire Festival''' (オロチョンの火祭り) is a {{ill|festival|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}} held as part of the events in {{ill|Abashiri|en|Abashiri|WD=Q305640|lt=Abashiri, Hokkaido|1=Abashiri}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|WD=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}.
The festival is a ritual performed to honor the spirits of indigenous peoples and pray for abundance. Participants dress in traditional clothing of northern minority groups and dance around flames to the accompaniment of {{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=drums|1=Drum}} and {{ill|khomus|ja|コロホル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/khomus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History == Abashiri, located along the {{ill|Sea of Okhotsk|en|Sea of Okhotsk|WD=Q41602|lt=Sea of Okhotsk|1=Sea of Okhotsk}}, has historically had active exchanges with northern peoples. At the {{ill|Moyoro Shell Mound|en|Moyoro Shell Mound|WD=Q11344562|lt=Moyoro Shell Mound|1=Moyoro Shell Mound}}, human remains of the {{ill|Okhotsk people|ja|オホーツク人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okhotsk people|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オホーツク文化|1=Okhotsk culture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1093651}}, some of whom became ancestors of the {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アイヌ|1=Ainu people|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q101828}}, have been discovered. In light of these historical connections, a festival called "Moyoro Festival" was held at the Moyoro Shell Mound around {{ill|1940|en|1940|WD=Q18633|lt=1940|1=1940}} to honor the spirits of northern peoples.
In {{ill|1950|en|1950|WD=Q18597|lt=1950|1=1950}}, after World War II, the festival evolved into the "Orochon Fire Festival," with cooperation from {{ill|Oroks|en|Oroks|WD=Q8426825|lt=Oroks|1=Oroks}} and {{ill|Nivkh people|en|Nivkh people|WD=Q60046|lt=Nivkh|1=Nivkh people}} peoples who had repatriated from {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|WD=Q7792|lt=Karafuto|1=Sakhalin}}. It was incorporated as an official summer festival of the city. Later, it became part of the "Comeback Salmon in Lake Abashiri" event held annually from {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=October 1|1=October 1}}, gaining popularity as a tourism attraction.

This legacy led to the naming of the festival as the Orochon Fire Festival. Notably, no ritual resembling a "fire festival" exists in the culture of the Orochon people as they are known today. == See Also == * {{ill|Oroqen people|en|Oroqen people|WD=Q1059053|lt=Orochon people|1=Oroqen people}} * {{ill|Lake Abashiri|en|Lake Abashiri|WD=Q572825|lt=Lake Abashiri|1=Lake Abashiri}}
